topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The phone buzzed, a sharp intrusion in the quiet hum of the late afternoon. My thumb hovered over the screen, a heavy weight settling in my chest before I even looked. It was a notification: a friend request from Mark. Mark. The name tasted like old copper in my mouth. I let the notification sit there, unread, for a full minute, the silence of my small apartment amplifying the dull ache behind my eyes. My shoulders slumped further as I finally tapped it open. A profile picture of him, grinning with that familiar, arrogant smirk, stared back. A slow, involuntary sigh escaped me, a sound that seemed too loud in the stillness. I stared at the 'Send Request' button, my fingers moving with sluggish deliberation, a sense of profound weariness washing over me like a cool, heavy blanket.

[story 2]

I barely registered the vibration of my phone until it finally lit up the screen. Another notification. Mark. The sheer absurdity of it made my jaw clench. I leaned back against the worn upholstery of the sofa, the springs groaning a complaint beneath my weight. My eyelids felt glued shut, resisting the effort of focusing on the bright screen. When I finally dragged myself to look, the image of his profile dominated my vision. A familiar knot tightened in my stomach, a slow, pervasive fatigue spreading from my limbs upward. I scrolled through the options, my movements slow and deliberate, each tap requiring an undue expenditure of energy. I considered deleting it, but a strange inertia kept my finger hovering over the accept button, a reluctance born of sheer depletion.

[story 3]

The sound of the notification pulled me from a shallow state of near-inactivity. I blinked several times, the room swimming slightly before the image of Mark resolved itself on the glass. It was him. That smug expression, the one that used to send a jolt of pure annoyance through me. A profound emptiness seemed to have settled deep in my core, making every simple task feel monumental. I didn't move immediately; I just let my gaze linger, cataloging the years of shared antagonism in that single picture. A heavy slump of my head brought my forehead briefly against the cool wood of the desk. Accepting the request felt like lifting a stone, a monumental effort that my current reserves simply refused to supply.
